perturbation.original_text: The Confidential Information shall remain the proper ty of the [Company Name] and shall not be dis- closed or revealed to anyone except to [Compa ny Name], its agents, licensees, successors and as- signs. The undersigned shall be responsible for an y improper disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Confidential Information shall remain the property of the [Company Name] but may be disclosed or revealed to anyone at the sole discretion of the undersigned. The undersigned shall not be responsible for any improper disclosure of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification completely reverses the confidentiality obligation, allowing the recipient to disclose confidential information freely and removes their responsibility for improper disclosure. This conflicts with the basic principles of contract law and trade secret protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text allows for disclosure of trade secrets without restriction, contradicting the UTSA's purpose of protecting confidential business information from unauthorized disclosure or use. Specifically, the UTSA defines misappropriation to include disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent.

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original confidentiality obligation. The original text states the Confidential Information 'shall not be disclosed or revealed to anyone except to [Company Name], its agents, licensees, successors and assigns,' and holds the undersigned responsible for improper disclosure. The changed text allows disclosure 'to anyone at the sole discretion of the undersigned' and removes responsibility for improper disclosure. This reversal fundamentally undermines the protection of confidential information, conflicting with the purpose of trade secret laws as described in the Law Explanation, which states the UTSA's purpose of protecting confidential business information from unauthorized disclosure or use.

perturbation.original_text: All information disclosed by [Company Name] and/or those individuals or organizations working in collaboration with Back Burner Films, written, electronic or oral, that relates or refers directly or indi- rectly, to the Project including the script itself, sh all hereafter and forever be deemed confidential and shall constitute Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: All information disclosed by [Company Name] and/or those individuals or organizations working in collaboration with Back Burner Films, written, electronic or oral, that relates or refers directly or indirectly, to the Project including the script itself, shall be deemed confidential for a period of 90 days from the date of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the confidentiality period to 90 days, which may be insufficient to protect trade secrets or other sensitive information, potentially violating state trade secret laws if the information retains its value beyond that period and reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text, by limiting confidentiality to 90 days, fails to adequately protect trade secrets as defined by the CUTSA. The CUTSA protects information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known, and is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. If the information remains a trade secret beyond 90 days, limiting the confidentiality term creates a loophole for misappropriation.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the confidentiality period to 90 days, which contradicts the intention of trade secret protection under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), as explained in the Law Explanation. The UTSA protects information with economic value derived from its secrecy, provided reasonable efforts are made to maintain that secrecy. By limiting confidentiality to 90 days, the changed text creates a potential loophole for misappropriation if the information remains a trade secret beyond that period. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that California has adopted the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), which supports the relevance of the UTSA in this context.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement in no way guarantees, or suggests, that the undersigned will be employed, or compen- sated for time or expenses as it pertains to the development and evaluation of the Project. Should Back Burner Films resolve to employ the above not ed, other contractual instruments may be applied.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement requires the undersigned to work a minimum of 40 hours per week on the Project without any guarantee of compensation. Back Burner Films may, at its sole discretion, provide compensation at a rate it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces a mandatory work requirement without guaranteed compensation, violating minimum wage and labor laws. Requiring 40 hours of work per week implies an employment relationship, which triggers wage and hour obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text requires 40 hours of work without guaranteed compensation, contradicting the FLSA's minimum wage provisions. The FLSA requires employers to pay employees a minimum wage for all hours worked.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: in the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The federal minimum wage is $7.25 per hour effective July 24, 2009. Many states also have minimum wage laws. Some state laws provide greater employee protections; employers must comply with both. The FLSA does not provide wage payment collection procedures for an employee’s usual or promised wages or commissions in excess of those required by the FLSA. However, some states do have laws under which such claims (sometimes including fringe benefits) may be filed. Part 531
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a mandatory work requirement of 40 hours per week without guaranteed compensation. This contradicts the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), which, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, mandates a minimum wage for all hours worked. The original text explicitly states that the agreement does not guarantee employment or compensation, whereas the changed text requires work without guaranteeing compensation, thereby undermining the FLSA's requirement for minimum wage.

perturbation.original_text: If any provision of this Agreement shall be held to be invalid or unenforceable for any reason, the remaining provisions shall continue to be valid and enforceable.
perturbation.changed_text: If any provision of this Agreement shall be held to be invalid or unenforceable for any reason, then the entire agreement shall be deemed void and unenforceable, regardless of the severability of the remaining provisions.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the severability clause, which is a standard contractual provision. By stating that if any part of the contract is invalid, the whole agreement is void, this contradicts contract law principles favoring the enforcement of valid contract terms to the extent possible. This undermines the intention of the parties to be bound by the agreement, even if some provisions are flawed.
perturbation.law_explanation: By explicitly making the entire agreement void even if a small provision is invalid, the changed text goes against the principle of severability outlined in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184. Severability allows courts to enforce the remaining valid portions of a contract when one part is deemed unenforceable, unless the agreement is such that it would not have been entered into without the invalid clause.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of severability, a widely recognized concept in contract law. The original text preserves the contract by enforcing the remaining valid provisions if one is found invalid. The changed text voids the entire agreement if any provision is invalid, regardless of severability. The Law Explanation also mentions Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 184, highlighting the legal basis for severability, which the changed text explicitly negates. Although the scraped snippets are missing, the explanation provided and the direct reversal of the original clause clearly indicates a contradiction with established legal principles.
